rename-and-remove-tracked-files.label: Rename and Remove Tracked Files
rename-and-remove-tracked-files.summary: Maintain tracked community-project paths safely and record one structural cleanup.

sketch0.title: Tracked Paths Change with the Project

try_rename_and_remove_tracked_files_sket.title: Rename the Tracked Schedule
try_rename_and_remove_tracked_files_sket.prompt: The project now calls its public schedule `events.txt`. Rename tracked `schedule.txt` to `events.txt` with Git so the path change is staged.
try_rename_and_remove_tracked_files_sket.hint: Use the Git-aware move for this tracked file; keep it in the repository root.

help.concept: git mv updates the working tree and stages the tracked rename.
help.hint_2: Do not copy the file and leave the old path behind.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf -- community-project
mkdir -p -- community-project
git -C community-project init -q -b main
git -C community-project config --local user.name 'ZoeSkoul Learner'
git -C community-project config --local user.email learner@zoeskoul.local
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s '# Community Project

Shared schedules and contact notes.
' > community-project/README.md
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community schedule

Saturday cleanup: 10:00 AM
' > community-project/schedule.txt
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community contacts

Help desk: 555-0100
' > community-project/contacts.txt
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Old planning notes

Spring flyer draft.
' > community-project/old-notes.txt
mkdir -p -- community-project/generated
printf %s 'Generated summary

Created automatically from project notes.
' > community-project/generated/summary.txt
git -C community-project add -A
GIT_AUTHOR_DATE=2026-01-01T12:00:00Z GIT_COMMITTER_DATE=2026-01-01T12:00:00Z git -C community-project commit -q -m 'Start community project'
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community schedule

Saturday cleanup: 9:30 AM
' > community-project/schedule.txt
git -C community-project add -A
GIT_AUTHOR_DATE=2026-01-02T12:00:00Z GIT_COMMITTER_DATE=2026-01-02T12:00:00Z git -C community-project commit -q -m 'Update community schedule'
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community contacts

Help desk: 555-0130
' > community-project/contacts.txt
git -C community-project add -A
GIT_AUTHOR_DATE=2026-01-03T12:00:00Z GIT_COMMITTER_DATE=2026-01-03T12:00:00Z git -C community-project commit -q -m 'Update community contacts'

try_rename_and_remove_tracked_files_sket.instructions: The project now calls its public schedule `events.txt`. Rename tracked `schedule.txt` to `events.txt` with Git so the path change is staged.

0.message: That action can discard or rewrite history. Use the safe, layer-specific workflow taught in this course.

1.message: Did you use Git to rename the tracked schedule file to `events.txt`?

try_rename_and_remove_tracked_files_sket.title: Remove the Obsolete Planning Notes
try_rename_and_remove_tracked_files_sket.prompt: The schedule rename is already staged. Remove tracked `old-notes.txt`, then inspect the staged name-status summary so both structural changes are visible.
try_rename_and_remove_tracked_files_sket.hint: Stage the obsolete-file removal and review the full cleanup proposal.

help.concept: git rm removes a tracked path from the working tree and stages that removal.
help.hint_2: The summary should show a rename and a deletion.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf -- community-project
mkdir -p -- community-project
git -C community-project init -q -b main
git -C community-project config --local user.name 'ZoeSkoul Learner'
git -C community-project config --local user.email learner@zoeskoul.local
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s '# Community Project

Shared schedules and contact notes.
' > community-project/README.md
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community schedule

Saturday cleanup: 10:00 AM
' > community-project/schedule.txt
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community contacts

Help desk: 555-0100
' > community-project/contacts.txt
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Old planning notes

Spring flyer draft.
' > community-project/old-notes.txt
mkdir -p -- community-project/generated
printf %s 'Generated summary

Created automatically from project notes.
' > community-project/generated/summary.txt
git -C community-project add -A
GIT_AUTHOR_DATE=2026-01-01T12:00:00Z GIT_COMMITTER_DATE=2026-01-01T12:00:00Z git -C community-project commit -q -m 'Start community project'
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community schedule

Saturday cleanup: 9:30 AM
' > community-project/schedule.txt
git -C community-project add -A
GIT_AUTHOR_DATE=2026-01-02T12:00:00Z GIT_COMMITTER_DATE=2026-01-02T12:00:00Z git -C community-project commit -q -m 'Update community schedule'
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community contacts

Help desk: 555-0130
' > community-project/contacts.txt
git -C community-project add -A
GIT_AUTHOR_DATE=2026-01-03T12:00:00Z GIT_COMMITTER_DATE=2026-01-03T12:00:00Z git -C community-project commit -q -m 'Update community contacts'
rm -f -- community-project/schedule.txt
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community schedule

Saturday cleanup: 9:30 AM
' > community-project/events.txt
git -C community-project add -- events.txt
git -C community-project add -A

try_rename_and_remove_tracked_files_sket.instructions: The schedule rename is already staged. Remove tracked `old-notes.txt`, then inspect the staged name-status summary so both structural changes are visible.

0.message: That action can discard or rewrite history. Use the safe, layer-specific workflow taught in this course.
Did you remove the obsolete tracked planning notes?

1.message: Did you inspect the staged structural summary?

try_rename_and_remove_tracked_files_sket.title: Commit the Structural Cleanup
try_rename_and_remove_tracked_files_sket.prompt: The rename and removal form one organization purpose. Record them with the message `Organize community project files`, then confirm that short status is clean.
try_rename_and_remove_tracked_files_sket.hint: Commit the prepared path cleanup and verify the current state.

help.concept: Related path maintenance can form one commit when it serves one cleanup purpose.
help.hint_1: Use Organize community project files exactly.
help.hint_2: The short status should be empty afterward.

try_rename_and_remove_tracked_files_sket.solutionCode:
git commit -m "Organize community project files"
git status --short

try_rename_and_remove_tracked_files_sket.instructions: The rename and removal form one organization purpose. Record them with the message `Organize community project files`, then confirm that short status is clean.

0.message: That action can discard or rewrite history. Use the safe, layer-specific workflow taught in this course.
Did you record the prepared structural cleanup?

1.message: Did you verify that no unfinished tracked paths remain?

single-choice-1.title: Purpose of git mv
single-choice-1.prompt: What is the primary purpose of the `git mv` command?
single-choice-1.hint: Focus on what the command or concept observes or changes at this point in the workflow.

help.concept: Use Git-aware structural operations, review the staged paths, and record a cleanup without losing earlier versions.
help.hint_1: Remove options that act on a different repository layer than the one named in the question.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer that remains accurate even when other unrelated files exist.

options.a: To move a file without tracking
options.b: To rename a file and stage the change

single-choice-2.title: Effect of git rm
single-choice-2.prompt: What happens when you use the `git rm` command?
single-choice-2.hint: Focus on what the command or concept observes or changes at this point in the workflow.

help.concept: Use Git-aware structural operations, review the staged paths, and record a cleanup without losing earlier versions.
help.hint_1: Remove options that act on a different repository layer than the one named in the question.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer that remains accurate even when other unrelated files exist.

options.a: The file is moved to a new location
options.b: The file is removed from the working directory and staged for deletion

multi-choice-1.title: Commands to Verify Staged Changes
multi-choice-1.prompt: Which commands can you use to verify staged changes before committing?
multi-choice-1.hint: Judge each option independently; choose only the statements that stay true in this exact repository state.

help.concept: Use Git-aware structural operations, review the staged paths, and record a cleanup without losing earlier versions.
help.hint_1: A partly true option is still wrong if it adds an effect Git does not guarantee.
help.hint_2: Use the layer named in the prompt—working tree, staging area, or history—to test each choice.

drag-reorder-1.title: Steps to Rename and Commit a File
drag-reorder-1.prompt: Arrange the steps to rename a file and commit the change using Git.
drag-reorder-1.hint: Start with the state that must exist before the next action can make sense.

help.concept: Use Git-aware structural operations, review the staged paths, and record a cleanup without losing earlier versions.
help.hint_1: Track how the project moves from working files to a proposed snapshot and then to history.
help.hint_2: Read the ordered steps as one continuous repository story.

tokens.t1: Use git mv to rename the file
tokens.t2: Check the status with git status
tokens.t3: Commit the change with git commit

sketch0.title: Tracked Paths Change with the Project
sketch0.bodyMarkdown: Projects evolve even when they contain only text files. `git mv` stages a tracked rename, and `git rm` stages a tracked removal. Git ultimately records the before-and-after snapshots.

sketch1.title: Structural Changes Need Review
sketch1.bodyMarkdown: `git diff --staged --name-status` summarizes staged additions, deletions, and likely renames. Review the path changes before recording them.

sketch3.title: Group One Cleanup Purpose
sketch3.bodyMarkdown: Renaming the schedule and removing obsolete notes serve one organization purpose, so they can share one focused commit.
